coredata.dc:description: During on-site nuclear rescue and treatment, efficiently carrying out decontamination operations and conducting rapid detection after decontamination are the key points in nuclear decontamination work. However, the existing decontamination and detection are divided into different steps and the decontamination effect needs to be judged by means of special instruments, which hinder the nuclear rescue work to a certain extent. To effectively solve this problem, a novel detergent (CQDs-DTPA) was developed by doping diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA) onto the surface of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through an amidation reaction. The decontaminating effect of CQDs-DTPA on UO2 2+ reached 76.8 %, which is better than that of the clinical certification DTPA (decontaminating effect of 71.3 %). CQDs-DTPA is highly selective and sensitive to UO2 2+, and fluorescence quenching occurs after 10 s of exposure. During the decontamination process, it can be found that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-DTPA increases with the removal of uranium and eventually returns to the initial value, which indicates the real-time monitoring role of CQDs-DTPA. In addition, safety experimental evaluations fully confirmed the safety of CQDs-DTPA in practical applications. This study indicated that CQDs-DTPA could serve as a radioactive uranium detergent with real-time detection of the decontamination effect, which could also provide a reference for developing other radionuclides' rapid decontamination agents.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: During on-site nuclear rescue and treatment, efficiently carrying out decontamination operations and conducting rapid detection after decontamination are the key points in nuclear decontamination work. However, the existing decontamination and detection are divided into different steps and the decontamination effect needs to be judged by means of special instruments, which hinder the nuclear rescue work to a certain extent. To effectively solve this problem, a novel detergent (CQDs-DTPA) was developed by doping diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA) onto the surface of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through an amidation reaction. The decontaminating effect of CQDs-DTPA on UO2 2+ reached 76.8 %, which is better than that of the clinical certification DTPA (decontaminating effect of 71.3 %). CQDs-DTPA is highly selective and sensitive to UO2 2+, and fluorescence quenching occurs after 10 s of exposure. During the decontamination process, it can be found that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-DTPA increases with the removal of uranium and eventually returns to the initial value, which indicates the real-time monitoring role of CQDs-DTPA. In addition, safety experimental evaluations fully confirmed the safety of CQDs-DTPA in practical applications. This study indicated that CQDs-DTPA could serve as a radioactive uranium detergent with real-time detection of the decontamination effect, which could also provide a reference for developing other radionuclides' rapid decontamination agents.

Schematic of CQDs-DTPA decontamination and real-time detection of radioactive uranium:(a)Preparation of CQDs and CQDs-DTPA.(b)Mechanism of fluorescence quenching by CQDs-DTPA in contact with uranium.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Uranium (U), as the most important radioactive actinide element at present [1,2], occupies an irreplaceable and crucial position in the fields of energy production and national defense [3]. With the increasing frequency of uranium usage, its resultant chemical toxicity and radiotoxicity have posed serious threats to the health and safety of humans and other biological entities [4]. Generally, radioactive uranium can enter the human body through inhalation, ingestion, skin contact, or wounds. Once inside the body, it can cause damage to certain organs (such as the liver and kidneys), the nervous system, the reproductive system, and the digestive system. And the skin, as the first protective barrier of the human body against external aggression, is always the first to be exposed to the nuclear environment [5,6]. Therefore, to prevent the intrusion of radioactive uranium into the human body through the skin, it is necessary to carry out surface decontamination as soon as radionuclides are contaminated on the body's surface. Currently employed decontaminants for removing uranium include hydroxyethylidene diphosphonic acid (HEDP) [7], ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA), calixarene nanoemulsions, 1.5 % sodium bicarbonate solution, and hydroxypyridinone compounds (HOPO) [8]. At the end of the decontamination work using these detergents, the decontamination effect must be judged with the help of special nuclide detectors. This type of detector has the significant advantages of being portable and accurate. Still, it also has some drawbacks, such as high prices, sensitive and easily damaged detection probes, and repeated testing will lead to excessive decontaminant use [9]. Also, the step-by-step decontamination and detection will lead to a large amount of radioactive wastewater that needs to be treated and reduce the rescue efficiency. Therefore, if real-time monitoring of the decontamination depth can be realized during the decontamination process, it can not only improve the efficiency of nuclear emergency work but also reduce the pressure of sewage treatment and the risk of secondary contamination of personnel after the nuclear emergency work.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of carbon nanomaterials with a particle size of <10 nm [10–12], which have attracted much attention for their unique properties, such as stable photoluminescence, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and simple synthesis routes [13,14]. In recent years, CQDs have demonstrated superior performance and irreplaceability as fluorescent probes for the detection of various heavy metal ions [15–18]. Liu et al. [19] successfully synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot photocatalysts for the fluorescence detection and photocatalytic degradation of nicotine wastewater using a hydrothermal method. The prepared N-CQDs detected nicotine wastewater with high sensitivity and a good linear range (1–50 mM). The photodegradation of nicotine wastewater by the catalyst was 99.69 % within 90 min. Zheng et al. [20] introduced abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of dopamine-modified molybdenum oxide quantum dots (MoOx QDs), and obtained DA-functionalized MoOx QDs (DA-MoOx QDs) with strong binding affinity for uranium. The sensitivity of DA-MoOx QDs is nearly 1000-fold higher than that of MoOx QDs quantum dots alone, with a detection limit of 3.85 nM, which is higher than that of most reported nanomaterials. The above reports indicate that adjusting or modifying the surface functional groups of CQDs can improve the fast response time and detection ability of CQDs for metal ions, and make CQDs adaptable to more complex detection, based on which we can design and construct a fluorescent material of CQDs, and use it for the detection of nuclides on skin.

Diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA) is the main chelating agent approved by the French Nuclear Safety Authority(ASN-2008) [21] and the U.S. National Council on Radiation Protection and Measurements (NCRP) [22] for removing contamination from the body surface of radionuclides used in clinical practice. It can be used to decontaminate the body surface of radionuclides by forming a complex with the radionuclide to form a stable compound with higher molecular weight. By forming complexes with radionuclides, it forms stable compounds with higher molecular weights to achieve the purpose of decontamination [23]. It is widely used in nuclear wastewater treatment, environmental decontamination, and medical treatment. However, as mentioned above, using DTPA for decontamination requires the use of instruments to judge the effect, which is time-consuming and laborious.

In this study, a novel and efficient radioactive uranium detergent (CQDs-DTPA) with integrated real-time monitoring and decontamination function was designed and synthesized based on CQDs and DTPA. Based on the material CQDs-DTPA, we have conducted in-depth studies on it in various aspects, covering material characterization, verification of the decontamination effect, and investigation of the quenching mechanism. The results showed that CQDs-DTPA was highly selective and responsive to uranium; the decontamination effect of CQDs-DTPA(76.8 %) was better compared with that of conventional DTPA(71.3 %), while the recovery of fluorescence intensity indicated that CQDs-DTPA was capable of real-time monitoring of decontamination. This experiment conclusively demonstrated that CQDs-DTPA has great potential for rapid decontamination and real-time monitoring of UO2 2+.

Anhydrous citric acid (CA), polyethyleneimine (BPEI), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid pentasodium salt (DTPA·5Na), cobalt chloride (CoCl2), strontium chloride (SrCl2), and cerium nitrate [Ce(NO3)3] were all obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., China.1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl) was sourced from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. Cesium chloride (CsCl) and uranyl nitrate hexahydrate [(N2O8U·6H2O)] were purchased from Shanghai Yien Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. All chemicals used were of analytical grade.

Epithelial fibroblasts were provided by the Chinese Academy of Sciences cell bank (clone 929, L929), and healthy male guinea pigs were selected from Keyu Animal Technology Co., Ltd., License No: SCXK (Beijing) 2021–0040. Male New Zealand rabbits were also sourced from Keyu Animal Technology Co., Ltd., License No: SCXK (Beijing) 2024–0008. Male Kunming (KM) mice were obtained from Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology Co., Ltd., License No: SCXK (Beijing) 2021–0040. All animals were acclimatized for at least one day under standard conditions (temperature: 25 °C ± 3 °C, humidity: 50 % ± 5 %, and a light-dark cycle of 12 h). The animal experiments received approval from the “Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals” by the National Institutes of Health and adhered to the guidelines set forth by the Animal Care and Welfare Committee at the Beijing Institute of Radiation Medicine (Beijing, China; IACUC-DWZX-2020-503).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using a hydrothermal method by dissolving 1.0 g of CA with 0.5 g of BPEI in 25 mL of hot water, which was then transferred to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h [24]. Following this, the solution within the reactor was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and filtered through a membrane with a pore size of 0.22 μm; subsequently, dialysis was performed with water being changed every 6–8 h on average. Finally, the resulting liquid was freeze-dried for three days to obtain a yellow powder product.

CQDs-DTPA conjugates were synthesized using EDC/NHS as coupling agents to successfully link amino groups on CQDs with carboxyl groups on DTPA molecules [25]. Specifically, after dissolving 0.5 g DTPA·5Na in ice-cold deionized water totaling up to 40 mL and stirring for half an hour, we added 0.12 g NHS. Subsequently, we added another portion containing EDC weighing approximately 0.19 g while continuing stirring under ice conditions for an additional half-hour period before removing it from the ice bath and allowing it to stand at room temperature for 2 h thereafter adding in about 0.3 g CQDs into the mixture, followed by reaction over 24 h duration. The unreacted starting materials were removed via filtration and finally subjected again to a dialysis process lasting three days utilizing deionized water. This led to obtaining brown solid material representing the final product known as CQDs-DTPA.

Transmission electron microscope(TEM) and high-resolution TEM(HR-TEM) were performed to observe the morphology of the CQDs and CQDs-DTPA by using a JEOL-JEM-2100 F operated at 200 kV. The particle sizes of CQDs and CQDs-DTPA were measured by utilizing the Zetasizer Nano ZS90 Malvern laser particle size analyzer. X-ray powder diffraction(XRD) measurements of the phase composition and crystal structure of CQDs and CQDs-DTPA were performed using a Rigaku SmartLab SE diffractometer with a test angle range of 5–90°and a scan rate of 10°/min, prepared by placing carbon nanotubes dissolved in hexane on a 1 cm × 1 cm silicon wafers. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs, CQDs-DTPA, CQDs-DTPA + UO2 2+and UO2 2+ were measured with a UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2600). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy(FTIR) was performed by using ZNHS-ZS10A FTIR spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy(XPS) was used to analyze the elemental composition and surface functional groups of CQDs and CQDs-DTPA by employing the Thermo Scientific-ESCALAB Xi+. Raman spectroscopy was performed to analyze the degree of graphitization of CQDs and CQDs-DTPA using a Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution with an excitation source at 532 nm in the 1000-2000 cm−1 range. Photoluminescence(PL) and quantum yield(QY) measurements were performed using a Hitachi F4600 spectrofluorometer. Cis-lifetime (UK-Edinburgh-FLS1000) studies on the mechanism of fluorescence quenching. The potential of UO2 2+, CQDs-DTPA and CQDs-DTPA + UO2 2+ were determined by using the Zetasizer Nano ZS90 Malvern laser particle size analyzer. The dynamic changes of chemical bonds in CQDs and CQDs-DTPA were compared and analyzed by using two-dimensional infrared vibrational echo spectroscopy(2D-IR). The elemental composition information of CQDs-DTPA at different depths was analyzed by X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES). The Stern-Volmer equation(formulas 1 and 2) is important for distinguishing between dynamic and static quenching. The detection limit was calculated using formula 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the decontamination capability of CQDs-DTPA
ce:section.ce:para:
The target area (2 × 2 cm) was delineated on the dorsal region of the guinea pig following hair loss; intraperitoneal injection of 3 % sodium pentobarbital was administered for anesthesia. A total of 40 μL of uranium solution (1 g L−1) was uniformly applied to the target area for 5 min, with 1 min dedicated to even application using a swab and the subsequent 4 min allocated for waiting. A 10 mL of different samples were used to rinse the contaminated area on the back, respectively. The rinsing liquid and peripheral blood were separately collected, and skin samples from the back were excised. Each experimental group consisted of five replicates. Subsequently, the samples were subjected to nitration and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. The concentration of the resulting samples was determined according to the quantitative ICP-MS method for uranium nuclides in two biological matrices, whole blood, and skin, established in the previous laboratory. which RF power 1150 W, RF matching 1.8 V, sampling depth 10 mm, concentric nebulizer, plasma flow rate 15 L min−1, acquisition mode TRA, Omega deflection voltage 105 V, Omega lens voltage 13.6 V, integration time 1.0 s [26]. The skin clearance rate (%) was calculated according to established formula 4.
(4)

rate

)

Where A is the mean value of skin residue in the blank group, ng·g−1; B is the skin residue in each treatment group, ng·g−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aggregation behavior of CQDs-DTPA under the presence of uranium
ce:section.ce:para: In an ultra-pure water medium, 2 mL of CQDs-DTPA (2 mg mL−1) was prepared, and its particle size distribution was measured using a Malvern laser diffraction particle size analyzer. Subsequently, the ultra-pure water was replaced with a uranium solution (4 mg L−1) while maintaining the same material concentration, and the particle size distribution was re-evaluated.

The cytotoxicity assay was conducted using the MTT method [27]. Mouse fibroblast cells (L929) were cultured with different concentrations of sample solutions for 24 h, 48 h, and 72 h. The absorbance values of each well were measured using a microplate reader to calculate the relative growth rate (RGR). Each sample was tested in six parallel wells. In the acute systemic toxicity experiment [28], Kunming mice were divided into three groups, with ten mice per group. The experimental group received a single intraperitoneal injection of 50 mL kg−1 of the sample, while the control group was administered a saline solution (0.9 % NaCl). Following administration, all mice were monitored for immediate reactions as well as recorded for signs of systemic toxicity and body weight changes at 4 h and 1–14 d. For skin irritation testing [29], skin responses in each group were counted according to Section 10: Types of Reactions in Biological Evaluation of Medical Devices. Observations and records regarding skin irritation at various experimental sites were documented.

Where A is the average absorbance value of the experimental group and B is the average absorbance value of the blank control group.

Statistical differences were analyzed by one-way ANOVA using GraphPad 8.0.2 software, and data statistics were presented as mean ± standard deviation. ∗P < 0.05, ∗∗P < 0.01, ∗∗∗P < 0.001. When ∗P < 0.05 means statistically different and ∗∗P < 0.01 is statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQDs and CQDs-DTPA
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with blue fluorescence were synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid as a carbon source and polyethyleneimine as a nitrogen source. Based on the synthesized CQDs, CQDs-DTPA nanocomposites were prepared by the amidation reaction of the amino group of BPEI on the surface of CQDs with the carboxyl group of DTPA units (Fig. 1 a and b).

The microstructures and morphologies of CQDs and CQDs-DTPA were characterized by TEM and HR-TEM, and it can be seen from Figs. S1a and 2a that the prepared CQDs and CQDs-DTPA are uniformly sized and well-dispersed rounded structures without obvious aggregation. The HR-TEM images show (insets of Figs. S1a and 2a) that the lattice spacings of CQDs and CQDs-DTPA are 0.41 nm and 0.39 nm [30,31], respectively, suggesting that the compositions of CQDs and CQDs-DTPA are similar to that of graphite, which corresponds to the graphitic carbon crystalline surfaces of (002). The particle size distribution of CQDs-DTPA is 3–10 nm with an average size of 9.52 nm (Fig. 2 b), which was slightly larger than the size of CQDs (8.73 nm). The increased particle size originates from the additional DTPA ligands linked to the CQDs surface. The phase and structure of the CQDs and CQDs-DTPA were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), as shown in Fig. 2c, CQDs and CQDs-DTPA have a diffraction peak at 2θ = 21.12°and 2θ = 22.7°, respectively, and the lattice spacings were calculated to be 0.41 nm and 0.39 nm according to Bragg's law, both of them corresponded to the graphitic carbon (002) crystal planes, which proved that the synthesized carbon dots had a graphitic-like structure [32]. The introduction of DTPA causes the (002) crystal plane diffraction peak of CQDs to shift to a higher Angle (Δ2θ = 1.58°), and the corresponding layer spacing is reduced from 0.41 nm to 0.39 nm, indicating that DTPA group enhances the interlayer π-π interaction and promotes the longitudinal ordered arrangement of the sp2 hybrid carbon domain. This structural evolution is consistent with the increasing trend of graphitization revealed by the decrease of ID/IG ratio in Raman spectra [33,34]. Raman spectrum can further reveal the structure and graphitization degree of the CQDs-DTPA. The Raman spectrum showed (Fig. 2d) two distinct peaks at 1356 cm−1 (D band) and 1574 cm−1 (G band). The G band corresponds to the graphitization in the CQDs [35], while the D band is associated with defects, edge effects, and functionalization [36]. The intensity ratio ID/IG = 0.86 demonstrates that the CQDs-DTPA own a stronger graphite degree than CQDs (ID/IG = 0.89), where the D peak shift is mainly because the carboxylic acid group (-COOH) and amine group (-NH) of DTPA can covalently bond with the sp3 defective sites (e.g., hydroxyl groups, edge dangling bonds) on the surface of CQDs to reduce the unsaturated defective states, and thus the D peak shifts, which promotes the transformation of more disordered carbon results to graphitic carbon [37]. Therefore, TEM, XRD, and the Raman spectrum indicate the crystalline nature of the carbon dots.

FTIR was used to determine the surface functional groups of CQDs-DTPA. The infrared results are shown in Fig. 2e, the intensive band in the region of 3300–3470 cm−1 indicates the absorption by stretching vibration of non-condensed terminal NH2 or NH groups and O–H [38,39]. The peak in the 2968 cm−1 range is due to C–H stretching [40,41]. The absorption peaks at 1700 cm−1, 1653 cm−1, and 1566 cm−1 correspond to characteristic amide bonds formed between free –NH2 groups on CQDs and –COOH groups on DTPA during the amidation reaction [25]. Additionally, a peak at 1400 cm−1 represents the C–N bond characteristic of DTPA [42]. Given that traditional FT-IR spectroscopy is difficult to effectively distinguish the dynamic changes of chemical bonds in CQDs and CODs-DTPA, this study employed two-dimensional correlation spectroscopy (2D-IR) to analyze the molecular vibration modes of the samples before and after functionalization. As shown in Fig. S7, in the synchronous correlation spectrum of CQDs-DTPA, four significant self-peaks were observed at 3286 cm−1, 1736 cm−1, 1612 cm−1, and 1400 cm−1, respectively, which are related to O–H, CO, N–H, and C–N structures. All cross-peaks were positively correlated (red areas), indicating that the molecular trends of the above functional groups were consistent with the enhancement of the corresponding functional group characteristic peaks in FT-IR, confirming that DTPA was covalently anchored on the surface of CQDs through amidation reaction [43].

XPS analysis was used to confirm the structure and presence of the constituent elements and the chemical composition of the CQDs-DTPA. The four characteristic peaks at 285.03, 399.3, and 530.97 eV indicate the presence of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s in the CQDs-DTPA, respectively (Fig. 2f). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2g) could be decomposed into three peaks at 287.32, 285.31, and 284.26 eV, corresponding to CO, C–O/C–N and CC/C–C, respectively [44]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2h) resolved peaks at 399.67 and 399.56 eV, attributed to N–H and C–N bonds, respectively [45]. By comparing the N 1s spectra of individual CQDs, it can be observed that the C–N content in CQDs-DTPA has increased(Fig. S3). This indicates that DTPA has been successfully grafted onto the surface of CQDs. Fig. 2i shows the fragmentation of the high-resolution XPS O 1s spectrum into four peaks at 532.43 and 530.8 eV, which are assigned to CO and O–H groups, respectively [46]. The nitrogen K-edge X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) was used for verification(Fig. S8), and it was found that the C–N content in CQDs-DTPA increased significantly, which was consistent with the XPS results. Meanwhile, the XPS results were consistent with the FT-IR results, indicating that DTPA had been successfully grafted onto the surface of CQDs. The CQDs characterization results are shown in Figs. S1–S5.

Then, the optical properties of CQDs-DTPA were analyzed using ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) and steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy (PL). The UV results are shown in Fig. 3 a, and it can be seen that there are two clear absorption spectra located at 250 and 353 nm. The 250 nm one originated from the π → π∗ transition of aromatic CC, and the 353 nm one is assigned to n → π∗ transition of aromatic CO [47,48]. PL detected that the maximal excitation wavelength of the CQDs and the CQDs-DTPA is 360 nm, and the maximal emission wavelength at this time is 445 nm (Fig. 3b), but the fluorescence intensity of CQDs after grafting DTPA decreased somewhat, mainly since DTPA itself was non-fluorescent, and its grafting damaged the surface passivation layer of CQDs to a certain extent. In the inset of Fig. 3b, CQDs-DTPA is shown as a transparent and homogeneous light yellow liquid under daylight, while it produces intense blue photoluminescence under UV light irradiation. The emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths (310–400 nm) were also examined (Fig. 3c), and the emission wavelengths remained unchanged with the increase of the excitation wavelengths, indicating that CQDs-DTPA has a good dimensional homogeneity and an excitation wavelength-independent property [49,50]. From the above FT-IR, XPS, UV, and PL results, it can be concluded that DTPA was successfully grafted onto the surface of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of CQDs-DTPA for UO2 2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The abundant functional groups on the surface of CQDs-DTPA confer a strong chelating ability towards metal ions. Therefore, the ion selectivity and responsiveness of CQDs-DTPA towards uranyl ions were investigated. Firstly, the fluorescence changes of CQDs and CQDs-DTPA after contact with uranium were examined under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 a and Table S1, CQDs-DTPA exhibited strong fluorescence, dramatically quenched with the addition of UO2 2+; the quenching efficiency was calculated to be 0.96. The fluorescence response of CQDs quenched by UO2 2+ was also measured to demonstrate the role of DTPA modification. It could be seen that UO2 2+ also quenched the fluorescence of the CQDs, but the quenching efficiency (QF = 0.88) was lower than that of the CQDs-DTPA, illustrating that DTPA modification facilitated UO2 2+ binding [20]. To survey the selectivity and interference of CQDs-DTPA in UO2 2+ detection, different ions were introduced into the CQDs-DTPA aqueous solution including Cs+, Sr2+, Th4+, Co2+ and UO2 2+ ions were used for comparative experiments. As shown in Fig. 4b, after the addition of test ions, only UO2 2+ and Co2+ caused the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-DTPA to be significantly quenched, while the intensity of other metal ions did not change significantly. However, Co2+ ions cause some interference, which may be caused by the specific interaction between Co2+ and the carboxyl group on the surface of CQDs-DTPA. This problem can be eliminated by selecting a suitable masking agent. EDTA is selected as the masking agent for Co2+ [51], it has a strong coordination ability for Co2+ (logKCo-EDTA = 16.5). As shown in Fig. S9, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-DTPA was quenched slightly by Co2+ in the simultaneous presence of EDTA masking, while the fluorescence signal was significantly quenched after adding UO2 2+. The results showed that EDTA could be used as a masking agent to eliminate the influence of Co2+ on UO2 2+ detection. In addition, when other metal ions coexist with UO2 2+(Fig. S10), UO2 2+ can still effectively quench the fluorescence of CQDs-DTPA, showing satisfactory anti-jamming ability. This is mainly attributed to the introduced nitrogen- and oxygen-containing groups, which act as excellent coordination sites for UO2 2+ [52,53], allowing the CQDs-DTPA to exhibit strong selectivity for UO2 2+. To further analyze the effect of UO2 2+ ions on CQDs-DTPA, the fluorescence spectra of CQDs-DTPA under different UO2 2+ concentration conditions were determined in this study, and the results are shown in Fig. 4c. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs-DTPA showed a decreasing trend in the UO2 2+ concentration range from 0 to 50 mg L −1 incrementally [54]. Further observation of Fig. 4d showed that the (F0–F)/F0 values of CQDs-DTPA showed a linear correlation with the UO2 2+ concentration in 0–10 mg L−1 concentration interval. The linear fitting resulted in a linear equation of y = 0.05913X+0.14865, with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9994. Following the 3σ criteria, the obtained limit of detection (LOD) is 2.67 μg L−1. Response time is significant for fluorescence detection systems, especially radionuclides, which need to get the detection signal quickly in case of radioactive leakage [55]. Therefore, the response time of CQDs-DTPA after contact with uranyl ions was then investigated, as shown in Fig. 4e and f. When uranyl ions were added to the CQDs-DTPA solution, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-DTPA was quenched within 10 s. With time, the experiment tested the fluorescence signals at different time points, respectively, and it can be seen that the fluorescence signals after quenching are very stable, and the response time is speedy, which can achieve real-time detection of uranyl ions. The above study concludes that CQDs-DTPA exhibits strong selectivity and sensitivity towards uranyl ions, with a rapid and stable response time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CQDs-DTPA for uranium decontamination and real-time detection
ce:section.ce:para: The decontamination ability of uranium was tested as a primary measure of the value of decontaminant use, and animal experiments were conducted to verify the decontamination of uranium by CQDs-DTPA. The decontamination effects of deionized water, CQDs solution, DTPA solution, and CQDs-DTPA solution on the skin surface contaminated with uranium were compared after 5 min of uranyl nitrate back staining. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the CQDs-DTPA solution showed the highest decontamination rate of 76.8 %, slightly higher than that of the DTPA group. The uranium concentration in whole blood was examined, and the results were all below the lower limit of quantification, indicating that the amount of uranium entering the blood in the case of intact skin was very small. Then, the uranium nuclide content in the decontamination solution was detected by ICP-MS (Fig. S6). The uranium content in the washing solution of the CQDs-DTPA group was detected to be 0.029 mg, while the uranium content in the washing solution of the DTPA group was 0.025 mg. Statistical analysis showed that the difference between the two groups was highly significant (p < 0.001). This result suggests that CQDs-DTPA exhibits a stronger decontamination ability to complex more uranium compared to DTPA. The result was in agreement with the efficiency of the removal of uranium from the skin. In summary, it can be seen that the CQDs-DTPA group has a stronger affinity for uranium than the other groups and can remove as much uranium as possible that has not penetrated the body.

At present, nuclides are detected by hand-held detectors at the site of a nuclear emergency, which can lead to reducing the rescue efficiency as a result of decontamination and detection are implemented step by step. If the decontamination progress can be monitored in real-time, it will greatly improve the rescue speed and save the rescue time. Based on the strong removal ability of CQDs-DTPA for uranium and the phenomenon of instant fluorescence quenching, it was subsequently verified whether the fluorescence change of CQDs-DTPA could grasp the decontamination progress, thus realizing the dynamic detection of the decontamination effect. To gain precise insight into the dynamic changes in fluorescence intensity during the purification process, the present study adopts a “micro-division” strategy to break down the purification process into several detailed steps. Specifically, 1 mL of CQDs-DTPA solution was divided into 5 equal parts, and then each part of the solution was used for the rinsing operation in turn. In this way, the real-time changes in fluorescence intensity during the rinsing process can be more clearly captured. As shown in Fig. 5b, we can see that the fluorescence intensity after the fifth washing is close to that of CQDs-DTPA without uranium exposure. Then, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs-DTPA solution in each wash was examined by PL in three parallel tests. The fluorescence results are shown in Fig. 5c. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs-DTPA in the first “micro-decontamination” process was significantly lower than its fluorescence intensity. During the second, third, and fourth subsequent washings, the fluorescence intensity of the wash solution showed a rapid increase as the uranium content on the skin surface gradually decreased. By the fifth collection, the fluorescence intensity was close to the initial fluorescence intensity level of the sample. These data suggest that dynamic monitoring of decontamination can be achieved by monitoring changes in the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-DTPA in the wash solution. In summary, based on the excellent optical properties of CQDs, the CQDs-DTPA formed by incorporating CQDs demonstrated the potential performance of both decontamination and real-time detection at the nuclear accident site. This feature can quickly and accurately remove contaminants and monitor real-time conditions during the rescue process, thus greatly accelerating the overall speed of the rescue operation and providing a more efficient solution for the emergency treatment of nuclear accidents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of CQDs-DTPA quick quenching to UO2 2+
ce:section.ce:para: Then the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CQDs-DTPA after contact with UO2 2+ was investigated. Generally speaking, the fluorescence quenching process refers to the interaction between fluorescent molecules and quenching molecules. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of CQDs mainly includes photo-induced electron transfer (PET) [56], internal filtration effect (IFE) [57], fluorescence resonance energy transfer(FRET) [58], static quenching and dynamic quenching [59]. The main means of confirming the type of quenching is to analyze the fluorescence lifetime, UV absorption spectrum, and fluorescence spectrum. Fig. 6 a shows the excitation and emission spectra of CQDs-DTPA with the absorption spectrum of UO2 2+, and it can be found that the broad absorption peaks of UO2 2+ are completely overlapped with the excitation spectrum (360 nm) and emission spectrum (445 nm) of CQDs-DTPA, which suggests that the fluorescence quenching mechanism that occurs after the contact of CQDs-DTPA with UO2 2+ may be related to the fluorescence internal filtering effect(IFE) [60]. Then the UV absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetimes were examined. The UV spectra of the CQDs-DTPA solution as well as the mixed solution of CQDs-DTPA and UO2 2+ were shown in Fig. 6b. With the addition of UO2 2+, the UV absorption peaks of CQDs-DTPA remained unchanged, and the preliminary speculation was that the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of UO2 2+ on CQDs-DTPA might be caused by dynamic quenching [61]. Further validation was then carried out by measuring the fluorescence lifetime. In the case of static quenching, the presence of the quencher does not change the fluorescence lifetime of the excited state of CQDs, i.e., τ0/τ = 1; however, the presence of the quencher in the dynamic quenching shortens the fluorescence lifetime of the excited state of CQDs. The results are shown in Fig. 6c and Table S2, indicating that the presence of UO2 2+ decreases the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs-DTPA from 8.82 ns to 7.47 ns (τ0/τ = 1.18). Therefore, the UV absorption peaks of CQDs-DTPA were unchanged after contact with UO2 2+. Still, the fluorescence lifetime was changed indicating that the bursting mechanism of UO2 2+ was consistent with the dynamic bursting. The quenching type was further verified by using the standard Stern-Volmer equation [62,63], and Kq was less than the maximum dynamic quenching value (2 × 1010 M−1 S−1), confirming that a dynamic quenching effect did occur between CQDs-DTPA and UO2 2+. Furthermore, Zeta potential analysis indicates (Fig. S11) that the surface potential of the CQDs-DTPA solution is −14.2 mV, while after coordination with UO2 2+, its Zeta potential significantly decreases to −4.27 mV. The reduction in the magnitude of the surface negative charge indicates two key phenomena. There is a strong electrostatic interaction between CQDs-DTPA and UO2 2+, and the weakening of electrostatic repulsion is a key factor inducing the aggregation of CQDs-DTPA [64]. It was confirmed by dynamic light scattering measurement that UO2 2+ can induce the aggregation of CQDs-DTPA, namely the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect (Fig. 6d), and the degree of aggregation is proportional to the concentration of UO2 2+.

Therefore, the main reason for the quenching of CQDs-DTPA upon contact with UO2 2+ is due to the introduction of DTPA, which is functionalized by the DTPA functional group, i.e., the hydroxyl group of DTPA can be coordinated with UO2 2+ to form a complex, a process that ultimately leads to the unique fluorescence quenching of CQDs-DTPA with the addition of UO2 2+ (Fig. 6e).

The above study shows that CQDs-DTPA has the advantages of good water solubility, strong decontamination effect, and real-time monitoring, so it has great potential for application in nuclear site decontamination and rescue. Still, to apply CQDs-DTPA to humans at the nuclear site, it is necessary to evaluate the safety of CQDs-DTPA to determine whether there are any potential safety hazards when applied to the decontamination. The cytotoxicity of CQDs-DTPA was determined by MTT method (Fig. 7 a), with the metabolism and value-added of L929 cells, the normal growth and proliferation of the cells after 72 h showed obvious value-added trend, and the relative cell proliferation rate of the experimental groups were all >75 % (the cell activity value of >75 % is considered that the medical device is in the safe range), which indicates that CQDs-DTPA has good cellular compatibility. The results of acute toxicity experiments are shown in Fig. 7b. After intraperitoneal injection of CQDs-DTPA, saline, and CQDs in the experimental Kunming mouse group, the weight of the mice tended to increase for 14 consecutive days, and the Kunming mice did not show any uncomfortable symptoms. They did not show lethal convulsions or other adverse reactions. The results of skin irritation experiments found (Fig. 7c) that obvious erythema and edema appeared in the experimental area of sodium dodecyl sulfate. At the same time, the sample group was consistent with the saline group and did not show obvious erythema and edema. The above safety experiments indicate that CQDs-DTPA has good biocompatibility and can be used for humans at nuclear sites.

In this study, we successfully synthesized a novel radioactive uranium detergent as CQDs-DTPA by introducing DTPA to the surface of CQDs through an amidation reaction. Based on our data, CQDs-DTPA has dual functions, which can not only effectively remove uranium from the skin surface but also the decontamination progress in real-time by fluorescence quenching or not. Uranium is removed from the skin surface mainly by chelation between DTPA and uranium, while fluorescence quenching is mainly ascribed to the CQDs-DTPA particle aggregation due to the above chelation. The results of the safety experiments showed that CQDs-DTPA has good biocompatibility and can be used on human skin surfaces. This study demonstrates the dual-functional potential of the developed material in radioactive uranium decontamination and real-time fluorescence-based monitoring. To further broaden its applicability, future research should focus on the following directions: (1) Material Optimization: Enhance fluorescence quantum yield and photostability through crystal structure modulation and surface engineering modifications. (2) Scenario-Specific Validation: Conduct rigorous testing under simulated nuclear emergency conditions, prioritizing material stability in complex radiation environments and establishing an integrated assessment framework with commercial α/β contamination monitors. (3) Functional Expansion: Develop multidentate ligand-grafted derivatives capable of recognizing transuranic elements (e.g., plutonium and americium), ultimately constructing an intelligent platform for broad-spectrum radionuclide decontamination and monitoring.
